next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Doug folded the same flannel shirt for the third time. The sleeves wouldn't square right, the cuffs refusing to line up, and he gave up and stuffed it into his duffel. His other shirts were already in there, along with the prescription bottles he'd moved from the upstairs bathroom Saturday morning. The Lexapro and the Ativan sat buried between a pair of jeans and his shaving kit, where they'd been since this whole thing started. An hour ago he'd taken his evening dose in the basement bathroom with the door locked, the way he'd taken every dose this weekend: quickly, quietly, the bottle opened and closed in a single motion so the rattle of pills wouldn't carry through the thin walls. Twenty-five years of friendship and he still couldn't swallow an antidepressant unless he was sure the coast was clear.

The great room had the feel of a waiting room where the appointment had been cancelled. Paul sat at the kitchen table with his reading glasses and his phone, texting Anne about departure times, pausing after every line to read it back before his thumbs moved again. Joel had his laptop on the couch, answering the work emails he'd been ignoring since Thursday, the blue light catching the sharp angles of his face. The draft board was still there, and nobody had suggested taking it down. Nobody had suggested much of anything today. The day had passed in the half-speed of an airport delay, too awake to sleep, too tired to do anything useful, suspended in the lag between catastrophe and departure.

Mallick hadn't been back since Sunday evening. No follow-up calls, no u...
open_hooks_to_carry: The fantasy football waiver wire debate: should Martin's auto-drafted team stand? Who manages his roster as tribute?
The argument becomes a proxy for loyalty, obligation, and what they owe each other and the dead
Nate observes: Tommy and Cliff sitting too far apart and being too polite
A last dinner, a sunset on the lake — the final moment that feels like the weekends that came before
This chapter must land as genuinely funny while the reader senses, consciously or not, that the comedy is about to end
prev_tail_excerpt:
...self what that meant.

The scar on his forearm had stopped itching. Both hands lay flat on the armrests, palms down, fingers spread. Tommy looked at them the way he looked at his players in film sessions when he wasn't judging technique but capacity. What could this body do if the call asked for it.

Cliff's phone lived in Cliff's pocket. The backup lived somewhere Tommy couldn't reach. A server. A building. A room with humming lights and people he'd never meet. Cliff, though, wasn't abstract. Cliff was a hundred and seventy-five pounds in loafers who called a treadmill a workout.

Tommy thought about the dock. Thirty feet of wood into the lake. Slight give under the planks. Swim platform at the end. Water beside it was three feet deep, maybe less where the silt mounded around the pilings.

Deep enough if a man went in hurt. Deep enough if another man kept him there.

The idea changed texture. It wasn't strategy now but weight, footing, timing. Cliff in loafers on wet boards with Tommy standing right there.

Out past the dock the loon called again. The lake had lost its copper and gone flat gray. Once the air dropped, the boards would sweat slick. A man in loafers on wet wood at night could lose his footing. People in the house wouldn't hear much if nobody was listening for trouble.

Tommy picked up the beer Cliff had brought him. The can was still half full. He tipped it over the rail and watched the spill flash once in the last gray light before it vanished into the pine needles.

He set the empty can on the armrest and wiped his hands on his shorts.

Then he went inside.
secondary_character_beats: Doug should be drinking water instead of beer — a small detail that matters later
Joel and Paul should exchange a look that signals they are beginning to compare notes
state_deltas_required: the group's desperate performance of normalcy while every relationship is fractured
the group's pretense of normalcy is audibly cracking — Nate observes the Tommy-Cliff tension — last comedy before the break
